original_abstract:
This paper provides a critical review and assessment of current approaches to linguistic rhythm, challenging the long-standing debate between phonetic evidence and phonological intuition. While traditional typography categorizes languages into stress-timed, syllable-timed, and mora-timed groups based on perceived isochrony, numerous phonetic studies have failed to identify consistent acoustic correlates or objective timing regularity in the speech signal. Furthermore, emergent phonetic models that treat rhythm as a byproduct of factors like syllable complexity or vowel reduction struggle to account for rhythmic variation between closely related dialects or the early acquisition of rhythmic patterns by infants.

We argue that rhythm is not merely a "phonetic illusion" but a fundamental mental framework essential for language processing and segmentation. Moving beyond strictly phonetic metrics, we propose that rhythmic types reflect deep-seated structural differences in prosodic organization. By drawing on metrical theory and experimental evidence from speech perception, this article suggests that each rhythmic class is defined by a specific primary unit of processing—the foot, syllable, or mora. Ultimately, we contend that a coherent theory of rhythm must be explicitly modeled within a phonological framework rather than viewed as a purely automatic consequence of phonetic circumstance.
humanized_abstract:
The aim of this paper is to examine the current state-of-the-art in the field of linguistic rhythm and contribute to the resolution of the age-old controversy over phonetic and phonological aspects of the subject matter. This paper first reviews the major typographical characteristics such as stress-timed, syllable-timed, and mora-timed rhythms based on isochrony and then discusses the majority of the phonetic research that has failed to find any objective correlates that could be considered as acoustic realization of rhythm. Additionally, new phonetic models that attempt to see rhythm as a secondary effect of some other factors, e.g. the complexity of the syllable and vowels reduction, are also discussed in relation to differences and similarities between dialects, and the fast development of rhythmic patterns in infant language acquisition.

According to our theory rhythm is not merely a “phonetic illusion” underlying human language processing and segmenting. Leaving aside strictly phonetic criteria and looking for more profound structural bases for rhythmic types, our article on the basis of metrical theory and a large number of speech perception experiments concludes that every type of rhythm is built around a different primary processing unit: the foot, the syllable or the mora. Consequently a rhythm theory must on no account be included in a purely phonological model — as the consequences are almost entirely mechanical — but has to be integrated into the more general framework of phonology.
